title: Measuring and Mitigating the Causal-Semantic Disconnect in Sparse Autoencoders
description: We propose the Intervention Fidelity Score (IFS), extending prior work on causal feature selection by combining necessity, sufficiency, and consistency components. IFS builds on Arad et al.'s output score (sufficiency) while adding necessity and consistency to better identify features for precise behavioral steering. We demonstrate that IFS-guided steering achieves comparable control with fewer side effects than both activation-based and output-score-based selection.
motivation: Sparse Autoencoders have become the dominant tool for neural network interpretability, but recent work reveals a troubling gap: SAEs optimized for reconstruction often fail to recover ground-truth features, and many semantically interpretable features have minimal causal effect on model outputs. While Arad et al. (2025) made significant progress with output scores for identifying steering-relevant features, their sufficiency-only metric may miss important aspects of causal influence: necessity (whether the feature is required for the behavior) and consistency (whether the effect is stable across contexts). There is no systematic method that combines these three components to distinguish features that merely correlate with concepts from those that actually drive behavior in a robust, context-independent manner.
proposed_approach: We propose three main contributions: (1) Intervention Fidelity Score (IFS), a three-component metric (necessity, sufficiency, consistency) that extends Arad et al.'s output score to comprehensively quantify how reliably a feature influences model behavior; (2) A systematic comparison showing how IFS differs from both traditional SAE metrics and sufficiency-only causal metrics; (3) Fidelity-Weighted Steering (FWS), a method that selects steering targets by full IFS rather than activation magnitude or output scores alone. We compute IFS efficiently using attribution patching (with validation against direct activation patching) and validate on GPT-2, Pythia, and Gemma-2 models using behavioral benchmarks.

related_work.title: Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models with Dictionary Learning
related_work.authors: Trenton Bricken, Adly Templeton, et al.
related_work.relation: Foundational SAE work; we build on their architecture and critique their evaluation metrics.

related_work.title: Sanity Checks for Sparse Autoencoders: Do SAEs Beat Random Baselines?
related_work.relation: Shows SAEs often perform similarly to random baselines; we extend this by measuring the causal-semantic disconnect directly.

related_work.title: Rethinking Evaluation of Sparse Autoencoders Through the Representation of Polysemous Words
related_work.authors: Gouki Minegishi, Hiroki Furuta, Yusuke Iwasawa, Yutaka Matsuo
related_work.relation: Demonstrates that MSE-sparsity Pareto optima may harm interpretability; complementary to our causal analysis.

related_work.title: Representation Engineering: A Top-Down Approach to AI Transparency
related_work.authors: Andy Zou, Long Phan, et al.
related_work.relation: Introduces activation steering; we improve steering precision through better feature selection.

related_work.title: Unintended Side Effects When Steering LLMs
related_work.relation: Documents steering side effects; our Fidelity-Weighted Steering directly addresses this problem.

related_work.title: Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Interpretable Causal Graphs in Language Models
related_work.authors: Samuel Marks, Can Rager, et al.
related_work.relation: Uses SAE features for causal circuit discovery; we focus on characterizing individual feature causal importance.

related_work.title: Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet
related_work.authors: Adly Templeton, Tom Conerly, et al.
related_work.relation: Large-scale SAE deployment; demonstrates practical steering but does not systematically evaluate causal importance.

related_work.title: Attribution Patching Outperforms Automated Circuit Discovery
related_work.authors: Aaquib Syed, Can Rager, Arthur Conmy
related_work.relation: Our method for efficiently computing causal effects via attribution patching, with validation against direct activation patching.

related_work.title: Analyzing (In)Abilities of SAEs via Formal Languages
related_work.relation: Shows many SAE features lack causal effects in controlled settings; we generalize and quantify this phenomenon.

related_work.title: SAEs Are Good for Steering – If You Select the Right Features
related_work.authors: Dana Arad, Aaron Mueller, Yonatan Belinkov
related_work.relation: Most closely related work. Introduces output scores (sufficiency) for feature selection. Our IFS extends this by adding necessity and consistency components, and we directly compare our full metric against their approach.

related_work.title: Improving Steering Vectors by Targeting Sparse Autoencoder Features
related_work.authors: Sviatoslav Chalnev, Matthew Siu, Arthur Conmy
related_work.relation: SAE-TS addresses steering side effects through targeted steering vectors. Our work complements this by improving feature selection, which can be combined with their vector construction method.

related_work.title: Evaluating SAE Interpretability Without Explanations
related_work.relation: Provides auto-interpretability scoring methods we use for comparing IFS against semantic interpretability metrics.

related_work.title: Relevance Patching: Faithful and Efficient Circuit Discovery via Activation Patching
related_work.authors: Ján Kramár, Matthew Farrugia-Roberts, et al.
related_work.relation: Discusses limitations of attribution patching (false negatives, approximation errors) that we address through validation.

related_work.title: Open Problems in Mechanistic Interpretability
related_work.authors: Nelson Elhage, Neel Nanda, et al.
related_work.relation: Outlines limitations of scalable causal methods including attribution patching approximations.

hypothesis: Ranking SAE features by Intervention Fidelity Score (IFS)—which extends Arad et al.'s output score by adding necessity and consistency components—will enable more precise behavioral steering with significantly fewer unintended side effects compared to both activation-based selection and output-score-based selection alone. Specifically, IFS will show stronger correlation with steering effectiveness than output scores alone, and fidelity-weighted steering will achieve ≥90% of target behavior change with ≤50% as many features and ≤70% the side effects of output-score-based steering.

confirming: IFS shows stronger correlation with actual steering effectiveness than output scores alone (Spearman ρ improvement ≥0.1)
Fidelity-weighted steering achieves ≥90% of target behavior change with ≤50% as many features as activation-based steering
IFS-guided steering has ≤50% the side effect score of activation-based steering and ≤70% of output-score-based steering
Component ablation shows that necessity and/or consistency components provide measurable improvements over sufficiency alone for at least some behaviors
refuting: IFS correlates similarly to output scores with steering effectiveness (difference in Spearman ρ < 0.05)
Fidelity-weighted steering performs similarly or worse than output-score-based selection
Necessity and consistency components provide no meaningful improvement over sufficiency alone

key_innovations: Extended causal metric (IFS): Three-component metric (necessity, sufficiency, consistency) building on Arad et al.'s output score
Systematic comparison: Comprehensive analysis of how different causal metrics correlate with steering effectiveness
Fidelity-Weighted Steering (FWS): Improved steering method using full IFS with direct comparison to output-score filtering
Component ablation analysis: Quantifying when and why necessity/consistency components matter beyond sufficiency

computational_requirements.approach: Use pretrained SAEs where available; focus on smaller models for exhaustive causal evaluation; validate attribution patching against direct activation patching on subset
